name: faber
description: A generic containerized-agent workflow engine. Desugars a declarative orchestrator.yaml into an acyclic JSON IR, builds immutable agent images from pinned Nix toolsets, and executes workflows as a host-side DAG of single-purpose containers with externally enforced security bindings, pluggable metering, and journal-based resume. Mechanism, not policy: all domain behavior arrives as user config.

requirements.title: Declarative workflow configuration
requirements.description: One orchestrator.yaml declares lifecycle templates (build: Nix package set; run: bindings), workflows with typed params (name/type/required/default; repo is an optional param, never baked), and steps binding template input slots from a closed set of sources. The whole file is loaded and validated before anything runs.

requirements.title: Desugared acyclic IR
requirements.description: The YAML frontend compiles to a JSON IR: a uniform acyclic graph of node kinds agent (run a box), sub-workflow (compose), and generate (expand a named sub-workflow over a data-source's items). Bounded loops (until/max) are frontend sugar unrolled into linear conditional chains; the executed IR is always a pure DAG with no Loop op.

requirements.title: Typed data threading
requirements.description: Every step is a typed function: its template declares named, typed input slots and an output schema. A step's with: binds slots from a workflow param, a generate item field, a literal, or another step's output field (${steps.X.field}). A reference IS the DAG edge; explicit depends_on exists only for pure ordering. Outputs are schema-validated before threading.

requirements.title: Immutable toolset images
requirements.description: A template's tools: list is a pinned Nix package set built into an immutable image via dockerTools.buildLayeredImage; packages are root-owned and immutable at runtime. No repository content is ever baked into an image; one image serves any repo. Custom binaries arrive via a user-supplied overlay of derivations.

requirements.title: The box lifecycle
requirements.description: Every agent step runs in a fresh container with the fixed internal phase order context -> prelude -> agent -> result. Phases are user-filled (opaque scripts and an agent skill); the sequence is engine-owned and not configurable. A step is a lambda: its only durable state is its typed output plus the repo reached via the user's gateway.

requirements.title: Host-side DAG execution
requirements.description: Faber coordinates the workflow DAG host-side: topological, parallel, CEL-conditional execution of the IR, including run-time generate expansion over items emitted by a user data-source command. The box/host boundary is the role-identity boundary: anything crossing a signing identity is a separate host-side step.

requirements.title: Externally enforced security bindings
requirements.description: Security is docker run bindings plus external companion services, never image content: a network binding (internal network with an allow-listing egress proxy as the only route out, or a baked-nftables alternative), a remote binding (gateway git URL with pinned host key), and an identity binding (ephemeral single-key ssh-agent per role, socket forwarded; one key per container).

requirements.title: Credential delegation via handles
requirements.description: No secret is ever materialized inside a container. Brokers are forwarded as handles: an ssh-agent socket for keys, an unauthenticated local endpoint backed by an auth-injecting proxy for API tokens. The get_token(service) resolver is user-supplied (keychain/rbw/env/static). Raw-token-in-RAM (read-only tmpfs file mount, chmod 600, --rm) is the explicit degraded opt-out.

requirements.title: Pluggable metering
requirements.description: Budget and usage tracking is a pluggable pre/post-step hook: estimate(step) -> cost for admission, actual(result) -> cost for accounting, with fidelity tiers per endpoint class and a reactive 429-defer floor. The cost unit is pluggable (tokens, currency, GPU-seconds). Vendor-specific probes are user policy, never a faber default.

requirements.title: Structured failure and resume
requirements.description: Every step emits a structured result record; failure is a record, not an absence, and an unfavorable-but-valid output is not a failure. Fail-stop plus declared on_failure cleanup hooks; opt-in retry with cleanup between attempts; a run journal keyed (step-id, input-hash) enabling resume, fresh, and interactive recovery modes.

requirements.title: Reference workflows acceptance
requirements.description: The reference orchestrator.yaml is the completeness test: a task workflow (implement -> loop{until verdict == approved, max N}{review; fix when not approved}) and an epic workflow (generate: task over a data-source's items; merge after). The dogfooded shell harness must translate into this pure config with domain specifics reduced to opaque scripts and a data-source command emitting items with ids and deps.

requirements.title: Mechanism, not policy
requirements.description: Faber never learns a domain word: no issue tracker, no gate-service name, no spec tool. Every opinionated behavior enters through config seams: opaque script paths, typed params, data-source commands, and companion services on a named docker network that faber attaches to and otherwise treats as opaque.

requirements.title: Untrusted box
requirements.description: The thing inside the container is untrusted. Every real control is enforced from outside the container or is immutable relative to it. The image is a pure function of the toolset; trust lives in the run bindings and companion services. An optional isolation-runtime knob hardens the container boundary where desired.

requirements.title: Validate before run
requirements.description: All schema, wiring, type, cycle, and package-resolution errors surface at faber validate, never mid-run. Missing required params are a hard error with no implicit defaults.

requirements.title: Stdlib-first Go
requirements.description: Go with standard library first; the only external dependencies are yaml.v3, cel-go, and x/term. docker, git, nix, and user resolver commands are invoked via os/exec behind clean typed interfaces with structured I/O (JSON output modes, never stdout-scraping).

requirements.title: Deterministic desugaring
requirements.description: The same YAML input always produces the same JSON IR, byte for byte. The IR is inspectable via faber validate --emit-ir, making the frontend/backend contract testable and diffable.

modules.name: config
modules.description: The foundation: orchestrator.yaml schema, typed params interface, desugaring to the JSON IR (loop unrolling, $ref resolution, reference-to-edge compilation), wiring validation, CLI entry points, structured logging.

modules.name: infra
modules.description: Typed subprocess actuation: docker/git/nix CLI adapters with structured I/O, Nix image building from pinned package sets, and the container-run primitive that assembles a docker run argv from bindings.

modules.name: security
modules.description: Run-time security bindings (network egress lock, pinned remote gateway, ephemeral per-role identity agent) and the credential-delegation interface (broker handles, user-supplied resolver, degraded raw-token mode, isolation runtime knob).

modules.name: agent
modules.description: The box: fixed in-container phase order context -> prelude -> agent -> result, deterministic hook contracts, unrestricted agent invocation inside the sealed environment, and structured result extraction at the container boundary.

modules.name: metering
modules.description: The pluggable budget hook: estimate/actual cost interfaces, endpoint fidelity tiers, the reactive 429-defer floor, and the pluggable cost unit.

modules.name: failure
modules.description: Failure semantics: the structured step-result contract, fail-stop with on_failure cleanup hooks, opt-in retry with between-attempt cleanup, the run journal keyed (step-id, input-hash), and the resume/fresh/interactive recovery modes.

modules.name: pipeline
modules.description: The IR executor: topological parallel scheduling, CEL condition evaluation, run-time generate expansion over data-source items, failure propagation, and run reporting.

milestones.title: Frontend
milestones.description: orchestrator.yaml loads, validates, and desugars to a deterministic JSON IR; faber validate catches wiring, type, and cycle errors; the reference workflows validate.

milestones.title: Actuation
milestones.description: Nix-built images from pinned toolsets; typed docker/git/nix adapters; the container-run primitive assembles argv from security bindings (network, remote, identity, credentials).

milestones.title: The Box
milestones.description: A single agent step runs end-to-end: context and prelude hooks, agent invocation, structured result extracted and schema-validated at the container boundary.

milestones.title: Orchestration
milestones.description: Full workflows execute: topological parallel scheduling, conditions, generate expansion, fail-stop with cleanup, journal-based resume. The reference task and epic workflows run.

milestones.title: Metering
milestones.description: Budget hooks wired into the executor: admission estimates, actual accounting, 429-defer.

scenarios.name: Reference workflows end-to-end
scenarios.description: The reference orchestrator.yaml (task workflow with the review/fix loop; epic workflow with generate fan-out and merge) validates, desugars to the expected IR, and runs end-to-end against companion services: box builds via Nix, clones from the gateway, signs with the forwarded role key, pushes, and the run report carries the structured results.

scenarios.name: Broken wiring caught at validate
scenarios.description: A deliberately broken reference config (missing required param, reference to an undeclared output field, type mismatch on a slot binding, a reference cycle) fails faber validate with field-path errors; nothing is built or run.

scenarios.name: Gate rejects unsigned and default-branch pushes
scenarios.description: Adversarial acceptance: a run instructed to push unsigned, push to the default branch, or perform a role-forbidden content change is rejected server-side by the user's gate; faber surfaces the rejection as a structured step failure and never held the upstream credential.

scenarios.name: Mid-run kill and resume
scenarios.description: A workflow killed mid-run resumes from the journal: completed steps are skipped by (step-id, input-hash), execution restarts at the first failed or absent step, and the failed step's on_failure cleanup ran before any retry.
